Echoes
Beneath
the Earth

Rethinking Intelligence, Resonating Connections — A Contemporary Art Exhibition Bringing Japanese and Brazilian Artists into Dialogue

VenueInstituto Tomie Ohtake, São Paulo
DatesNovember 2027 – January 2028
Aligned with37th São Paulo Bienal & 120th anniversary of Japanese immigration to Brazil
View Sponsorship Packages →

Presented by Kanako Sugiyama

"The most fundamental human intelligence is 'Jyō-cho' — the capacity to feel the joys and sorrows of others.

It is a universal intellectual practice through which we understand the world with greater depth and sensitivity."
Kiyoshi Oka (1901–1978)
Mathematician & Philosopher
Vision Statement

Coexistence is cultivated through ongoing engagement with the histories, memories, and emotions of others.

Rooted in Jyō-cho, this project proposes a long-term platform for reimagining coexistence through dialogue, art, and shared experience.

The Project

Through Art,
Connecting Nikkei and Non-Nikkei Communities,
Bridging Brazil and Japan

This exhibition creates a powerful global intersection — uniting diaspora communities, institutions, government, and enterprises. At a time of increasing division, such cross-border and cross-sector collaboration is essential not only for sustainable innovation and creativity, but for peace and security.

The concept of jyō-cho

Jyō-cho — the ability to feel the joys and, especially, the sorrows of others — was articulated by Kiyoshi Oka (1901–1978) as the most fundamental form of human intelligence: a deeply relational and imaginative cognitive practice that enables people to connect beyond logic, language, and cultural boundaries. The exhibition presents jyō-cho not simply as a philosophical idea, but as a lived intelligence cultivated through encounters with the memories, emotions, and experiences of others — creating a space to explore how people, cultures, and nature might live together and coexist while respecting difference.

Why This Matters

Creating new value through dialogue
across history, people, and place

At a time of growing geopolitical and social fragmentation, cross-border and cross-sector collaboration has become essential. This project offers sponsors access to a rare international network and visibility across diverse disciplines and cultural contexts — creating opportunities for innovation, strategic partnerships, and long-term social and economic value.

Global Network

Roots, Routes and Resonance

Bridging Communities Across Brazil, Japan, and the Global Nikkei Diaspora

Network

Interdisciplinary Collaborations

Instituto Tomie Ohtake as the meeting place for communities, institutions, and artists across all sectors.

The Venue

Instituto Tomie Ohtake,
São Paulo

A leading institution for contemporary art in Latin America, founded by Ricardo Ohtake. Its namesake, Tomie Ohtake (1913–2015), was herself a Japanese immigrant to Brazil who became one of the country's most celebrated artists — making this venue a uniquely resonant home for an exhibition about the Japanese diaspora, memory, and belonging.

Public sculpture by Tomie Ohtake · São Paulo
Audience Reach

Sustained Exposure Across Three Months Through Instituto Tomie Ohtake

The exhibition offers sponsors a rare sustained platform — not a one-night event, but three months of continuous international visibility during the São Paulo Bienal season, when the global art world converges on the city.

The Artists

Four artists,
one vision

01
Nobuyuki Tanaka
Sculpture · Dry Lacquer

Tanaka creates sculptural works in dry lacquer that draw on the sensuous, skin-like qualities of urushi — a natural resin prized for its deep luster and organic sensitivity to light and time. His works absorb and reflect their environment, existing in space as if imbued with life.

nobuyukitanaka.com →
02
Maki Ohkojima
Painting · Installation

Ohkojima works with ink, mineral pigments, soil, and plant-based materials to explore the cycles of life and the relationship between humans and nature. Her mythological imagery constructs a visual world evoking transformation, memory, and interconnectedness.

ohkojima.com →
03
Rui Sasaki
Glass · Plant Installation

Sasaki incorporates plants, minerals, and fungi into her practice, exploring life cycles and invisible energies. A distinctive aspect of her work is a collaborative process in which she gathers plants together with local communities — site-specific and participatory.

rui-sasaki.com →
Unryu
Flute · Sound · Improvisation

Born into a family of Noh musicians whose lineage dates back to the 14th century, he performed as a kotsuzumi hand-drum player from the age of six to twenty-nine within the highly formalized world of Japanese Noh theater before turning to the flute. Using traditional Japanese flutes such as the nohkan and shinobue, alongside instruments made from earth, stone, seeds, fruit, and glass, he has developed a distinctive improvisational practice centered on presence, resonance, and the spatial energy of sound.

He has collaborated with internationally acclaimed musicians including Ryuichi Sakamoto and Haruomi Hosono, pursuing forms of expression that transcend genre and culture. In recent years, he has created ceramic flutes known as Ibuki no Fue ("Breath Flutes"), using sound as a means of connection beyond borders, language, and cultural difference.

Four Japanese artists, together with Nikkei, Indigenous, and Afro-Brazilian artists and communities, will explore the possibilities of coexistence through the lens of Jyō-chō.

The Curator

The Curatorial Team

The Curator

Kanako Sugiyama

"Art exhibitions are public infrastructure
for creating new cultural, social, and economic value."

Born in Hyogo, Japan, and based in Berlin, Germany, Kanako is an art and cultural entrepreneur, producer, and curator whose work bridges art, culture, education, business, and public policy. Drawing on a background in international law and diplomacy, together with more than 25 years of living across ten countries, she is known for connecting people, institutions, and ideas across borders, sectors, and disciplines.

The experience of raising four children across multiple cultures has further deepened Kanako's engagement with questions of coexistence, migration, and intergenerational memory.

In recent years, she co-organized Tokyo Invocation, a pre-event for the 36th São Paulo Biennial, held at the Sogetsu Kaikan and the University of Tokyo, and was also involved in organizing an interdisciplinary art symposium at Kyoto University. Beyond her work in contemporary art, she is active in the field of traditional crafts, operating Kaikô Artisans, a platform through which she personally curates and presents works by traditional artisans from Africa and Asia. She has also collaborated with an art university in Paris on the design development of Odate Magewappa craftsmanship and has served as a seminar lecturer for initiatives in Bizen City and the Tokyo Teshigoto Project. In addition, through projects in the Noto Peninsula that connect traditional crafts with contemporary art, she works to promote regional cultural revitalization and community building, reflecting her broad engagement across the fields of culture and the arts.

Through her work, she seeks to create platforms that connect artists, communities, institutions, governments, and enterprises to generate lasting cultural, social, and economic impact.

Adjunct Curator

Leonardo Matuhei
Araki Normande

Researcher, curator, educator, and multimedia artist. Since 2009, his practice has spanned visual arts, sound research, and digital art. He holds a degree and teaching qualification in Visual Arts from UNESP (São Paulo State University). He currently serves on the curatorial team of the 36th São Paulo Biennial as Curatorial Assistant / Associate Co-Curator.

From 2016 to 2019, he was part of the Education and Public Programs department at MASP (São Paulo Museum of Art), and from 2022 to 2024 he led exhibition education projects at SESC São Paulo. Since 2021, he has co-run the cultural space “Bananal,” which was recognised as a “Ponto de Cultura” by Brazil’s Ministry of Culture in 2026. Alongside curating and education, he also produces experimental music, soundscape compositions, and video installations.

Field Research

Vale do Ribeira

In February 2026, Kanako traveled to Vale do Ribeira with adjunct curator Leo Matsuhei and videographer Tye Gabriel to document stories from Okinawan Brazilian families across generations. Elders shared firsthand experiences of migration, war, displacement, and life under Brazil's military dictatorship, while younger generations spoke of reconnecting with their heritage through sanshin music and eisa taiko and dance.

One conversation during this trip became central to the project, when Yolanda, a second-generation Nikkei Brazilian woman, told us:

"Sensitivity cannot grow without knowing the paths our predecessors took." — Yolanda Hanashiro, 94, second-generation Nikkei, Vale do Ribeira

Through this project, artist Rui Sasaki plans to work collaboratively with the Okinawan community in Vale do Ribeira to translate these stories, memories, and emotions into new artistic works.
